title: The Fastest Way to 1,000 Subs in 2026

transcript.text: Everyone who starts a new YouTube channel eventually ends up asking the same question. How many videos do I have to make until something actually happens? If you happen to look around, you might find channels that seem to have reached 1,000 subscribers after only uploading like three videos, and you'll also find channels that seem to have uploaded 1,000 videos, but only have like three subscribers. So, we wanted to see if we can get an answer to this question once and for all. How long does it actually take to grow on YouTube? Our data set included 200,000 YouTube channels that were created in 2025, hit 1,000 subscribers, and we broke them down by the number of uploads and the types of uploads that were posted to the channels. The picture this created was a lot more complicated and revealing than one single number could ever capture. The first question we wanted an answer to was, does the format you upload on YouTube matter? Conventional wisdom says, yes. You start posting shorts to kind of get yourself going, build momentum, and then once you have that momentum, you pivot into uploading more long-form videos. That's the general strategy being taught right now, and it kind of makes sense. There are YouTube shorts being pushed everywhere. YouTube seems to be encouraging people to make them. The algorithm is rewarding people for making them, so why wouldn't you start there? Well, assuming you're watching this because you want more subscribers, here's what we found. We split our 200,000 channels into buckets based on what they primarily uploaded. Channels that were at least 95% long-form hit 1,000 subscribers at a median of 46 uploads. Channels that were at least 95% shorts hit the same milestone at a median of 90 uploads. So, nearly double the amount of uploads for the same result. But, that reasoning does make a little more sense when you dive a [music] bit deeper into how people actually use each format. Let's start with the baseline. Before shorts, before the feed, before infinite scrolling, before these strategies that are being taught right now, that's what YouTube was. You would sit down, you'd watch a video, if you liked it and wanted to see more, you would subscribe. But now, viewers who scroll through the shorts feed are just in a completely different mindset. Something catches their eye, they watch it, and they move on. They likely don't even have enough time to notice your channel name in the bottom corner. There's no time for a viewer to develop that sense of who a creator is, what they're about, or if they want more from this voice. I want you to think about the last time you subscribed to a new channel on YouTube, and really think about it. There was almost certainly a moment, either a specific video or the specific way something resonated with you, that made you stop and go, "Yeah, I want more of this." That moment almost never happens within just the span of the first 30 seconds someone ever finds your content. People require more time to figure out who somebody is, and that's where long-form videos come in. When someone sits down and watches a 12-minute video from a creator they've never heard of before, and then they finish the video, that is a completely different feeling. Maybe that video changed their perspective on something, or maybe they learned something new, or maybe it just brightened their day when they needed it the most. That feeling can be a very powerful motivator to actually hit the subscribe button. A short can absolutely be the video that gets people to see your channel for the first time, but deciding to click on one short versus the entire subscribe button, it's just two different decisions. And >> [music] >> the data is pretty clear on which format tends to win out. And here, look, I'll show you our own channel data to prove this one. We looked at our shorts and long-form videos to net subscriber rate. Shorts gained over 3 million views, and our long-form videos had around 977,000 views in the same time frame. But when you look at the amount of subscribers we netted per view, long-form converts about 106 subscribers per view, and with shorts, it takes about 505 views per subscriber. Long-form is 4.7 times more effective at turning a viewer into a subscriber. All of these videos, shorts and longs, appeal to the same type of viewer, but at the end of the day, they're still two different audiences, and they all have different intentions. But if you just want a TLDR, generally speaking, the data shows that shorts get you views and long-form gets you subscribers. And you might be thinking, "Well, shorts take less time to produce." And yes, that is true, but you're still publishing twice as many to reach the same milestone. And each one tends to convert subscribers at a fraction of the rate. So, the math, while it can, doesn't always work out. And to be clear, this is not some giant argument about how you shouldn't have shorts existing on your channel. They can absolutely get different people to find your channel who would have never found it otherwise. But that leads us to our next important question. If just making shorts or just making long-form videos isn't the answer, then what What doing both? This mix of making both types of videos we'll call a hybrid channel, and it's a pretty logical approach. Upload shorts for each, and upload long-form for depth. And at the end of the day, you're covering all the bases and not leaving anything on the table, which seems like the smartest play, right? If we look under the hood though, the data surprisingly doesn't agree. Channels we classified as truly mixed, where neither format made up more than 80% of uploads, hit 1,000 subscribers at a median of 108 uploads. That's more than either of the pure strategies we just covered. And more than a third of mixed channels needed over 200 uploads to get there, which is the highest rate of any bucket we looked at. So, why does doing both tend to slow you down? Well, the short answer is that a hybrid channel can cause confusion. When you're posting a short one week and then a long the next week, there's a pretty good likelihood that you yourself haven't fully decided what your channel is yet. That uncertainty isn't just reflected in your upload schedule, because if somebody happens to watch a long-form video from you and they really like it, only to go to your channel page and find that it's full of shorts for the most part, now they don't know what to expect. If they subscribe, what are they subscribing to? A long-form channel that occasionally post shorts? A shorts channel that once in a while uploads the occasional long-form video? If your upload consistency is causing confusion for your viewers, then that's just going to make it all the more difficult for them to decide to hit the subscribe button. There's also a fear that this can confuse the algorithm as well. The way the algorithm works is simple. It needs to know who you're for, what you make, and who else watches channels like yours. When it has that picture, it knows who to put your channel in front of. And when it doesn't, it can't recommend you confidently. And remember from before, shorts audiences and long-form audiences aren't the same people watching the same way. Mixing your formats can potentially pull in two different audiences that have interests that may not overlap. The takeaway here is that hybrid channels aren't forbidden or completely ineffective. [music] But what makes a hybrid channel successful? Well, the answer to that question is like so many questions on YouTube. Are you ready? It depends. Well, good luck. See you later. I'm kidding. When we cut the hybrid channel bucket apart and go a little bit deeper, we see that the more long-form leaning a channel is, the faster it reaches 1,000 subscribers. So, the closer your hybrid channel looks to being a long-form channel that just posts shorts occasionally, the closer the results look to a long-form channel. But, the more important factor between fast-growing hybrid channels versus the ones who struggle is whether or not there's a content bridge. This is what we see creators in our comment section struggle with all the time. They upload a short that gets tens of thousands of views, and then their next long-form video gets virtually no views at all. That gap is what the content bridge can solve. The viewers who are seeing the shorts are simply not crossing over to the long-form videos. Many creators experience this, and they keep posting shorts hoping that the volume will hopefully be enough to eventually get some people to cross over to the long-form [music] videos. But, volume isn't the issue. It's usually that the shorts and the long-form videos simply do not have enough to do with each other. And there's a common misconception when it comes to the relationship between shorts and long-form content. People think that the short-to-long-form content bridge is a built-in feature on YouTube. When in actuality, that's something that you have to build yourself. Take this channel, Aviation Tech. They hit 1,000 subscribers in just 31 uploads. That's pretty fast. They post a short about the F-35 fighter jet, then a bit later on, a long-form video drops about the exact same plane. If you look at the rest of their shorts and their long-form videos, you see that their shorts are about the exact same topics as their longs. Every short is functioning basically as a trailer for a video that's already on the channel or coming soon. Now, compare that to this channel, Scary Stories 666. It took 219 uploads to get to 1,000 subscribers. They got there, but with much more output. Their shorts are all specific genres, backrooms and Japan scary myths content. They post some views over a couple thousand a piece. But, look at their long-form videos. When they talk about the same topic, in this case backrooms, they have good outliers on their channel. But, when they stray into generic or other topics, they have a hard time getting any traction. That's the content bridge problem in its simplest form. It's not necessarily that this creator lacks effort. They're posting consistently. But, since there's no direct link between their shorts and their longs, they're taking a lot longer to gain an audience. So, how do you actually build a content bridge? Well, the simplest way to think about it is that your shorts should make someone curious enough to want to watch the longer version of that thing. If your short can function as a trailer or a teaser or open a loop that can only be closed with a long form video, then you've created a content bridge. It's important the viewer should still get something out of the short that you've just made though. Don't upload shorts simply begging people to go watch a long form video. Make your video draw enough curiosity where people want to go watch the long form without you even having to ask. A gaming channel known as RK or ARK, I hope I'm saying it right, does this as well as any other channel we found. DayZ content. It's a survival game with lots of PvP encounters and unpredictable moments. Their shorts are single scenes. They all have one punchy narrative less than a minute long. Their long form videos scratch the same content itch but with more room to breathe. A viewer who finishes an RK short wants more of that feeling. So, they know exactly where to go. And since last year, RK went from 37,000 subscribers to nearly 80,000 subscribers because they used the content bridge effectively. Now, this doesn't mean that every short you make needs to be a companion to a long form piece of content, but it does mean that all of your shorts and all of your longs should feel like they were made by the same creator for the same type of audience and they're all about the same things. So, how many videos does it actually take to grow on YouTube? Well, if you just want numbers, here you go. Long form, median 46 uploads. Shorts, 90 uploads. Hybrid without a content bridge, 108 uploads with the most variance of any group. But, the numbers were never really the point. The channels that grew the fastest were simply the ones that figured out who they were the earliest. Whatever direction you want to go in, you need to make a conscious decision. And if your numbers are already looking way higher than the ones we've just put here, then it might be time to recalibrate. Because the channels growing the fastest right now are the ones following a very similar strategy and not a lot of people are talking about it. You're going to want to watch this video next to learn what it is.
